chapters.title: Depression is common, and you can recover
chapters.text:
Many people in the world today do not even know they are living with depression. The world runs on pressure and busyness, and that pressure makes life harder than it has to be. People chase money, fame, and success so hard that they rarely stop to take a real breath.

In trying to keep up with the endless demands, people try to do many things at once, and that kind of multitasking has driven most of them close to breaking point. Even computers crash when you ask them to do too much.

The 21st century is a lot. It moves fast, demands a great deal, and carries a steady hum of fear. Keeping up with it is hard, and that leaves little room to figure out what really matters. Many people walk through their days feeling worn out, but it does not have to stay that way.

This summary is meant to help anyone take charge of their mind. The chapters ahead will help you get to know yourself better, and with practice, you can learn to manage your own thoughts.

chapters.title: Why the feeling of emptiness drives us
chapters.text:
Humans are their own worst taskmasters. We carry a built-in engine that pushes us to achieve and keep moving. Everyone has a kind of selfish gene, the leading force that drives each person to press on. Parents add to that pressure, too. Every complaint and correction sticks in a child's mind. These voices can wear a person down, but they can also help them push through obstacles.

A lot of people put on a show of being busy and important. They do it to cover up the inner feeling of being useless, so they run from one task to the next. They run because they do not want to stop and look at the emptiness inside.

Evolution has shaped our brain to help the body survive and reproduce, not to chase happiness, and the brain has plenty on its plate.

Many people act busier than they really are. Society worships busyness, so everyone tries to play the part.

Some people try to find love and approval through shopping, buying things they do not really need.

Clothing, designer or not, is a way of showing who you are. Another big marker is the answer to the question, what do you do? People carry their identity on business cards, and new job titles keep popping up to label abstract roles.

Each person believes they have a purpose somewhere inside, but most of the time that belief is a kind of wishful thinking. In truth, most people still have a long emotional road to walk.

chapters.title: Unmet desires keep us from seeing our blessings
chapters.text:
A few emotional shortfalls are common to many people, and they are worth knowing.

Envy is one. Sometimes people want what they do not really need. Whatever it is, they want it, and they may even wish harm on the person who has it.

Rage is another. Anger is widespread in the human mind. A calm person can turn into an unrecognizable fury in a flash.

Deception is part of being human. People know how to bend situations in their favor. A person who wants to block someone else can wreck a job offer, a relationship, or a deal, and humans are animals under the skin, doing whatever it takes to get by.

Facial expressions can also fool those around us. Evolution gave people a range of expressions to throw others off the scent. People laugh even when they are nowhere close to happy.

Jealousy is not just a kid's feeling. Adults feel it just as strongly, even when they keep it hidden.

People's wants run a wide range, from basic needs like food, water, and shelter all the way to a private jet with a hot tub. The failure to get what we want leaves us stuck in a loop of longing.

Humans are given the basics for life, like breath, sight, hearing, and a mouth to talk and eat. Even with all that, many people suffer, and the cause is usually negative thinking.

Even when they are not chasing the next thing, humans spend a lot of time waiting for what is about to happen.

chapters.title: Mental illness is real and does not pick its victims
chapters.text:
Depression can sound exaggerated, but it is a real problem. Many people are at the mercy of their own strong feelings, and that is often how depression takes hold. Depression does not care whether you are famous, live in a hut, or come from any culture. It can land on anyone.

Depression is not sneaky, either. The signs are usually there to see, in the eyes of the person who is suffering, but it takes a careful look to spot them. For that reason, teachers should be trained to watch for early signs in students and to get them help before things get worse.

The way a person sees the world comes from what the brain is doing. The brain and the mind, including moods, emotions, thoughts, memories, and personality, are deeply linked. Depression is what happens when something goes wrong in the brain. Other brain-related conditions like prosopagnosia, Capgras syndrome, synesthesia, somatoparaphrenia, and Cotard's syndrome also come from brain trouble. Sadly, depression is often brushed aside.

A lot of people do not know that depression shares the same brain circuits and symptoms as many other conditions. Most of these conditions are seen as neurological glitches that change the way a person acts, which is why the stigma around mental illness is unfair.

Mental illness can be set off by genes, by disease, by trauma, or even by a physical injury.

Depression deserves to be taken seriously. Therapy and medicine help, but a big part of the cure is knowing that you are not alone. A person with depression needs to understand that they are sick in the same way people are sick with a physical illness.

It is common for people to think of a depressed person as self-indulgent, self-obsessed, or just looking for pity or an excuse to avoid responsibility. Loved ones may want to help, but they cannot really understand what it feels like. The best support often comes from people who have been through the same kind of struggle, because they are the only ones who truly know what depression feels like.

chapters.title: The brain shapes everything we do
chapters.text:
The way our neurons, or brain cells, are wired together shapes the way we think. Everything a person can do, including dreams, hopes, fantasies, and fears, comes from the connections between neurons, the chemicals they release, and the specialized regions of the brain. Those neurons, chemicals, and regions are tuned by a person's genes, by the way they grew up, by the society they were born into, and by the influence of their parents.

Every thought, feeling, and perception leaves a mark on the brain, and those marks are how we learn. The brain is not just a pile of undifferentiated processors, though. It has regions that do specific jobs. Neurons fire off chemicals called neurotransmitters, and those chemicals form pathways and networks that carry out specialized functions. Those pathways allow the different brain regions to talk to one another.

Our genetic code, written in DNA, tells every cell in the body what to do.

Along with neurons, genes play a major part in shaping who we are. No one knows exactly how much credit genes deserve, and it is not clear how much environment adds on top, but the two together decide the kind of person we become.

chapters.title: Nobody sees the world as it really is
chapters.text:
A lot of people think they see the world exactly the way it is. Nobody likes to admit that the picture in their head is a little warped, but it is true.

Humans live in a kind of virtual reality, because the world is not actually what we see.

Think of the visual cortex in your brain as a projection room that builds up what is out there from the signals it receives. In reality, you see the world in a series of snapshots, and it is your brain that ties them together to feel like smooth motion.

Around 70 different areas of the brain work together to build one picture of the world. Different parts handle color, movement, edges, shapes, and shadows. No single part of the brain gets the whole picture. In other brain regions, those images get a name, an association, or an emotional tag.

Nobody gets a full view of the world. All we get are small clips that drift through our awareness, and while we focus on one, a trillion other things are happening without us noticing.

The brain keeps the human heart beating over 100,000 times a day, pushing about two gallons of blood a minute through a network of blood vessels that stretches around 60,000 miles.

chapters.title: Stress is harmful to both body and mind
chapters.text:
When the body feels stress, a chain of reactions kicks off. The thalamus, the brain's relay station, sends a wake-up call to the brain stem. From there, signals go out to the major organs and muscle groups, getting them ready to fight or run. The adrenal glands release stress hormones.

Cortisol lowers the immune system's response to keep swelling down after any injury, and it fires up the amygdala so you stay alert. That boost in alertness creates more cortisol in a feedback loop. Cortisol also slows the hippocampus, which reduces memory so you focus on what you did the last time you faced a similar threat. It also shuts down digestion and lowers sex drive, since thinking about food or intimacy during a crisis would only get in the way.

Epinephrine, another chemical, makes the heart beat faster, moves more blood, and dilates the pupils, sometimes leaving you feeling light-headed.

All of that chemistry is useful if you are truly in danger. If you are not in a life-or-death moment and those chemicals keep flowing, they start to damage the body and brain. They can kill off neurons in the hippocampus, which leads to lasting memory loss.

Oxygen and blood are sent out to the arms and legs to get them ready for action, but if they stay there too long, the brain runs low on air and fresh blood, and clear thinking falls apart. In the worst cases, the result is a coma, and it can be even worse than that.

Over time, the excess chemicals wear down the immune system, leaving you open to viruses of every kind. They also lower serotonin, which leaves you joyless and pulls you into depression.

If the chemical storm keeps going, it can lead to heart disease, hardened arteries, type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers.

Stress, along with the chemicals it sets loose, will quietly break you down mentally and physically. The good news is that you can learn to manage these chemicals, boosting the ones that help you and lowering the ones that hurt you.

chapters.title: Mindfulness sharpens the mind and lifts depression
chapters.text:
With mindful practice, you can learn to take the reins of your mind and guide it where you want it to go.

Mindfulness is not complicated. All you have to do is pay attention. When you focus on what is happening right now, you begin to notice that thoughts are not facts. They are patterns that come and go, change, scatter, and fade.

The point of mindfulness is to teach yourself that thoughts are your servant, not your master.

A few simple exercises can help you get the hang of taming your mind.

The first is called RAIN. It stands for Recognize, Accept, Investigate, and Non-identification. Start by recognizing the feeling, whether it is fear, anger, or sadness. The next step is to let go of blame and shame. Then, send your attention to where the feeling sits in your body. Is your chest tight, or is there a churn in your stomach? Once you have noticed that, the next step is to step back from the feeling. That distance helps you regulate yourself. You will not snap out of sadness in a moment, but you will give yourself room to explore it.

The second exercise is called stop and notice. It only takes five seconds. The idea is to pause in the middle of a task and observe your mind and body. Notice what is going on inside, then carry on. Try this at the start of small activities like brushing your teeth, taking a shower, taking out the trash, or doing the laundry.

The third exercise is to focus on your feet. Sit or stand with your feet on the floor, your spine straight but not stiff. You can close your eyes or lower your gaze. Pay attention to the feeling of the ground under you. Notice the weight, the temperature, the tingling, or the lack of any feeling at all. When your mind wanders, take note of where it went and bring your attention back to your feet.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Life is full of uncertainty, and there is very little that anyone can truly control. Holding on tight to anything is pointless, because it only causes more pain.

To survive, the brain tricks you into believing the world is full of fixed patterns and that people can lay down permanent plans. The brain is always chasing past moments and trying to manage them, but that does not really work. It is better to learn to manage your own thoughts instead.

If your thoughts are running the show, they will slowly squeeze you with bitterness. You will become harder, more judgmental, and less tolerant, and the world will feel smaller and duller.

You have the power to manage your attention and build a path toward health and happiness. All it takes is lowering your reactive habits and growing more positive emotions.

Broadening the way people see themselves and the world should be the next stage of human growth. Doing so will widen human awareness and expand the choices people have, because they will be able to pick the right path before they act. People can grow wiser, make choices that pay off in the long run, and take action that benefits the future.

Putting mindfulness into practice will do a lot of good for everyone. It can affect inequality, the climate crisis, poverty, and disease. Mindfulness can help build a world where it is not the strongest who survive, but the wisest.

Try this

Surprise yourself and break a habit. Each small change in habit chips away at the wall you have built around yourself. Before long, you will be able to break out of the prison you made.

Did you make it to the end?
